title: Algorithmic Trading Circuit Breaker
spl:
index=trading sourcetype="risk:circuit"
| search state IN ("HALT","THROTTLE","KILL")
| stats latest(_time) as tripped, values(strategy_id) as strategies by gateway, state
| join max=1 gateway [| search index=trading sourcetype="fix:session" | stats latest(heartbeat_gap) as gap by gateway]
| where gap>30 OR isnotnull(tripped)
description: Ensures volatility/gateway breakers trip correctly and strategies are flattened when limits hit.
value: Ensures volatility/gateway breakers trip correctly and strategies are flattened when limits hit.
implementation: Log all manual overrides; post-mortem each halt with P&L and message counts.
visualization: Timeline (breaker events), Table (gateway state), Single value (strategies disabled).
knownFalsePositives: Algorithmic throttling and kill-switch drills from risk operations; these can look like live breaks during controlled tests.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Risk engine, exchange gateway.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="risk:circuit"`, `sourcetype="fix:session"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Log all manual overrides; post-mortem each halt with P&L and message counts.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=trading sourcetype="risk:circuit"
| search state IN ("HALT","THROTTLE","KILL")
| stats latest(_time) as tripped, values(strategy_id) as strategies by gateway, state
| join max=1 gateway [| search index=trading sourcetype="fix:session" | stats latest(heartbeat_gap) as gap by gateway]
| where gap>30 OR isnotnull(tripped)
```

Understanding this SPL

**Algorithmic Trading Circuit Breaker** — Ensures volatility/gateway breakers trip correctly and strategies are flattened when limits hit.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="risk:circuit"`, `sourcetype="fix:session"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Risk engine, exchange gateway. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: trading; **sourcetype**: risk:circuit. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=trading, sourcetype="risk:circuit". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by gateway, state** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• Filters the current rows with `where gap>30 OR isnotnull(tripped)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search on recent production data, spot-check a handful of results against the source system or operational runbooks (cash operations, EHR, payment gateway, etc.), and adjust thresholds to match your seasonal patterns and test windows so teams are not flooded with noise.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Timeline (breaker events), Table (gateway state), Single value (strategies disabled).
grandmaExplanation: In Financial Services, we pay close attention to "Algorithmic Trading Circuit Breaker" in our day-to-day work so that fraud, waste, and compliance issues do not slip by quietly. We help teams focus on the few situations that need a person, not every routine blip in the data.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable for the primary search: it relies on application- and industry-specific log formats, not a normalized CIM data model. Optional mapping to CIM, if you adopt it, is a separate follow-on project.
